questions.question: Why can diffusion training pick any timestep t directly instead of simulating the forward Markov chain step by step?
options: Because the composition of many Gaussian additions still yields a Gaussian, giving a closed-form q(x_t|x_0) = N(sqrt(alpha_bar_t) x_0, (1 - alpha_bar_t) I) that can be sampled in one step
It is an approximation that ignores the intermediate Markov steps
Because x_t is statistically independent of x_0 at any t
It is required by PyTorch's autograd tape to avoid graph cycles
questions.explanation: Repeated Gaussian additions close up analytically. The cumulative product alpha_bar_t encodes 'how much signal is left after t steps', and you can sample x_t directly from x_0 without running the chain. This closed form is what makes DDPM training O(1) per step rather than O(T) and is implemented in q_sample as a single multiply-add.

questions.question: Using `x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon`, suppose alpha_bar_t = 0.04. Approximately what does x_t look like relative to x_0?
options: Nearly identical to x_0 because alpha_bar is still a positive value
Almost entirely noise: sqrt(0.04) = 0.2 retains only 20% of the original signal while sqrt(0.96) ≈ 0.98 contributes nearly a full unit of Gaussian noise
Exactly zero because the signal and noise terms cancel at small alpha_bar
A perfect copy of epsilon with no remaining trace of x_0
questions.explanation: sqrt(0.04) = 0.2, so only 20% of the original pixel values survive in x_t. sqrt(1 - 0.04) = sqrt(0.96) ≈ 0.98, so the noise term contributes almost its full standard deviation. At alpha_bar_t near zero (late diffusion steps), x_t is nearly indistinguishable from pure N(0,I). This is what precompute_schedule precomputes and q_sample applies in one line.

questions.question: In `TinyUNet.forward`, the time projection `t_proj` is added to `h2` at the bottleneck rather than injected at the input `x`. What advantage does mid-network time injection provide?
options: Adding at the input is technically invalid because the channel count does not match the time embedding dimension
Adding at the bottleneck shortens the gradient path for the time embedding during backpropagation

The bottleneck features carry the richest semantic content; injecting t there lets the network scale its entire understanding of the scene by noise level before decoding, which is more effective than conditioning on low-level input features that do not yet represent objects or textures
PyTorch requires time embeddings to be added only at stride-2 layers
questions.explanation: The bottleneck has the smallest spatial size and the most concentrated semantic representation. Injecting timestep embedding there allows the model to modulate its global scene understanding (is this t=100 where fine detail matters, or t=900 where only coarse structure is visible?) before decoding. Production DDPM models inject sinusoidal time embeddings at multiple U-Net depths for even finer control.

questions.question: The DDPM `train_step` minimises `F.mse_loss(pred, noise)`. What exactly do `pred` and `noise` represent in this loss?
options: pred is x_{t-1} predicted directly by the model; noise is x_t, the noisy input the model received
pred is the full clean image x_0 reconstructed from x_t; noise is the beta schedule value at step t
pred is the model's estimate of alpha_bar_t; noise is the true alpha_bar value from the precomputed schedule

pred is epsilon_theta(x_t, t), the model's estimate of the noise added to x_0 to form x_t; noise is the actual epsilon sampled from N(0,I) and used in q_sample — the loss drives the model to predict exactly the noise it must reverse
questions.explanation: DDPM trains the network to predict the noise epsilon rather than x_0 or x_{t-1} directly. Given the noise estimate, the sampler computes x_{t-1}'s Gaussian mean analytically using the precomputed schedule. This epsilon-parameterisation simplifies training to a plain MSE loss and gives more numerically stable gradients, especially at large timesteps where x_t is near-pure noise.

questions.question: You plot `alpha_bar_t` for a linear beta schedule with T=1000 and see it drops to near-zero by t=600. Why does this matter for training quality?
options: It does not matter; all timesteps contribute equally to the MSE loss regardless of alpha_bar_t
PyTorch requires a linear schedule; non-linear ones cause numerical instability
alpha_bar_t must reach exactly zero at T for the sampler to terminate cleanly

The last 40% of timesteps (600-1000) add almost no new signal; training on them teaches the network to denoise near-pure noise, wasting model capacity; cosine or sigmoid schedules spread the signal-to-noise drop more evenly and improve sample quality especially at low step counts
questions.explanation: By t=600 on a linear schedule alpha_bar_t is already so small that x_t is essentially pure Gaussian noise regardless of x_0, giving the model no informative gradient. The cosine schedule (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021) maintains more signal at mid-range timesteps, which is why it is now the default for production diffusion models and why linear schedules are only used in the original DDPM baseline.

questions.question: Why does time conditioning in `TinyUNet` use a sinusoidal embedding of t rather than a one-hot vector across all T timesteps?
options: A sinusoidal embedding is smooth in t, so the network can interpolate between timesteps and encodes scale information at multiple frequencies that can be added to feature maps at any depth
One-hot vectors at T=1000 are too large to allocate on most GPU hardware
Sinusoidal embeddings are a requirement of the DDPM sampler loop and the beta schedule computation
One-hot encoding would violate the chain rule during backpropagation through the time branch
questions.explanation: Sinusoidal embeddings give the network a continuous, unique representation of every timestep using sine and cosine waves at different frequencies. Nearby timesteps have nearby embeddings, allowing the model to generalise across all T values. A one-hot encoding would produce 1000-dimensional sparse vectors with no structural relationship between adjacent timesteps — the network would have to memorise each independently. The idea is identical to transformer positional encoding.
